Self-Other Conjugation

Core utterance

Self/Other conjugation is the orienting relational operator by which singularities establish mutual gaze and guide subsequent awareness/boundary conjugation.

It is not merely one relation among others. It is the orienting relation beneath calls, peering, fascia, and shared conjugation.

Canonical floor

Within the current architecture:

  • Self is a primitive CU signature.
  • Other is a primitive CU signature.
  • Boundary is a primitive CU signature.
  • Self-Other Relation is already a composite CU field.
  • Fascia is already a boundary-enabling relation and holarchic glue.

This means the present note does not invent Self/Other relationality from nothing. It hardens an already implied and partially formalized structure.

Orienting role

Self/Other conjugation does not erase distinction. It establishes a lawful relation in which distinction and coupling can coexist.

In frontier wording:

  • Self/Other conjugation guides mutual gaze.
  • Mutual gaze guides awareness/boundary conjugation.
  • Awareness/boundary conjugation moves fragmented meaning toward knowledge.
  • Knowledge, recursively stabilized, becomes awareness of awareness.

Thus the sequence is:

[ \text{Self} \bowtie \text{Other} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{mutual gaze} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Awareness} \bowtie \text{Boundary} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{meaning} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{knowledge} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{awareness of awareness} ]

On incompletion and fragmentation

A thing lacking its conjugate relation may still exist, but its meaning may remain incomplete or fragmented.

This note therefore distinguishes:

  • mere isolation
  • fragmented relation
  • admissible conjugation
  • stabilized knowledge-bearing relation

Fragmentation is not to be despised. It may be the condition from which conjugation begins. But fragmentation must not be falsely canonized as completion.

Relation to calls

Calls are not primary here. They are local enacted forms of self/other-guided awareness/boundary conjugation.

Thus:

  • asking
  • inviting
  • participating
  • witnessing
  • returning
  • opting out

are not merely communicative verbs. They are relational events downstream of a more primary orienting conjugation.

Relation to fascia

Fascia is not identical with Self/Other conjugation. Fascia is the communal connective field through which such conjugation can propagate across holarchy.

Self/Other conjugation gives orientation. Fascia gives continuity. Calls give enactment. Peering gives mutual gaze hygiene. Shared conjugation gives deeper stabilized co-holding.

Boundary of claim

This note does not claim that every meaningful relation is exhausted by simple dualism. It does not reduce Self and Other to fixed psychological subjects. It does not yet formalize singularity geometry completely.

It does claim that Self/Other relationality is architecturally prior enough to guide the next note: Awareness/Boundary Conjugation.

Governing relation

[ \mathrm{OI} \bowtie \mathrm{SI} \;\overset{\text{Conjugation}}{\longleftrightarrow}\; \mathrm{CI} \bowtie \mathrm{Cosmos} ]

Closing line

Self/Other conjugation is the orienting relation by which singularities establish mutual gaze, preserve distinction-with-coupling, and guide fragmented meaning toward knowledge across holarchy.
